title: Physical Integrity Rights Score
subtitle: The score captures the extent to which citizens are protected from government killings, torture, political imprisonments, extrajudicial executions, mass killings and disappearances. Large positive scores mean abuses are rare relative to other countries and years, large negative scores that abuses are relatively widespread.
caption: The 2019 Physical Integrity Rights Score map displays the extent to which citizens are protected from government killings, torture, political imprisonments, extrajudicial executions, mass killings, and disappearances across the globe. The color scale ranges from dark red, representing a score of -4 where abuses are relatively widespread, to dark blue, representing a score of 4 where abuses are rare relative to other countries and years. The highest scores appear in the dark blue band across western and northern Europe, including Norway, Sweden, and Iceland, as well as in Canada, New Zealand, and Uruguay. Intermediate positive scores in the light blue band cover the United States, Australia, Japan, and much of South America, including Brazil and Argentina. The lowest scores, falling in the dark red band, are visible in a concentrated cluster across North Africa and the Middle East, specifically in Algeria, Libya, Egypt, and Yemen. A second area of low scores appears in South Asia, with India and Myanmar falling into the negative range. Large portions of sub-Saharan Africa, including Sudan, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Somalia, also register in the negative bands. Russia and China both fall into the light orange band, indicating negative scores between -1 and 0. Greenland and several countries in central Africa are shaded with diagonal lines, indicating no data is available for those regions. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of high protection scores in the Americas, Oceania, and Western Europe, while the lowest protection scores cluster in North Africa, the Middle East, and parts of Asia.
